ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term specific to Indonesian bakso; closest match is generic meatball soup food product (FOODON_03311737).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKM (Meat soup) is the closest defensible code; no bakso-specific FoodEx2 entry exists in the published hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept for bakso or Indonesian beef meatball soup identified in the published SNOMED CT international release.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of bakso weighing approximately 450 g, comprising jumbo beef meatballs, broth, and garnishes.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Meatball soup, beef, homemade (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Indonesian bakso exists in USDA FoodData Central, HPB Foodo, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. The closest approved-source equivalent used is USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for beef meatball soup (homemade preparation). Input nutrient values from the GrabFood listing (ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-checked against USDA SR Legacy data for beef meatball soup and found broadly consistent with the macronutrient profile (energy ~80–90 kcal/100g, protein ~6–7 g, fat ~3–5 g, carbohydrate ~4–6 g). Input values have been retained as the best available estimate with medium confidence. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, vitamin A, B-vitamins, vitamin D, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water are not fully reported for this adapted entry and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for bakso (Indonesian beef meatball soup) is available in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 52 based on the analogous entry for mixed meat and starch-thickened soup with tapioca-starch-based meatballs from Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). The carbohydrate contribution is primarily from tapioca starch (moderate GI ~54) and minor noodle components. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (52 × 25.02) / 100 ≈ 13.0, rounded to 13.1. Carbohydrate per serving = 5.56 g/100g × 450 g = 25.02 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from beef broth, soy sauce, and chilli sauce
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 266.7 mg/100 g yields approximately 1200 mg per 450 g bowl, approaching 50–60% of the 2000 mg daily limit recommended by Singapore HPB; patients with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce and chilli sauce, ask for broth on the side, and limit portion size to manage sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load from tapioca starch in meatballs and broth thickeners
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 52 (low–medium) and a glycaemic load of approximately 13 per 450 g bowl, bakso poses a moderate glycaemic challenge; patients with Type 2 Diabetes should control portion size, retain the protein-rich meatballs, and avoid added sugar-containing chilli sauces to limit postprandial glucose excursions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from beef and trans fat from processing, combined with high sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Saturated fat at 1.78 g/100 g and trans fat at 0.11 g/100 g are moderate concerns for cardiovascular disease; the high sodium content (~1200 mg per bowl) further elevates cardiovascular risk, and patients should limit frequency of consumption, request reduced broth, and substitute beef meatballs with leaner protein options where possible.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Arachidonic acid from red beef meat amplifying omega-6 inflammatory pathways
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Beef meatballs contain arachidonic acid which can amplify omega-6-driven inflammatory pathways relevant to rheumatoid arthritis; substituting beef meatballs with chicken or fish meatballs and retaining the cabbage (anti-inflammatory cruciferous vegetable) is advisable for RA patients.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bakso is a popular Indonesian beef meatball soup widely consumed across Indonesia and Southeast Asia, including Singapore where it is commonly sold at hawker centres and food courts. The dish consists of large, chewy, handmade beef meatballs made from a blend of ground beef and tapioca starch, which gives them their characteristic springy, bouncy texture. The meatballs are served in a clear, savoury beef bone broth seasoned with garlic, shallots, white pepper, and salt. Common accompaniments include yellow noodles or glass noodles, tofu puffs, fried shallots, spring onions, soy sauce, and sambal chilli sauce. Per 100 g, bakso provides approximately 84 kcal, 6.7 g protein, 4.0 g fat (1.78 g saturated), 5.6 g carbohydrate, 0.67 g dietary fibre, and 267 mg sodium. A standard 450 g bowl delivers roughly 380 kcal and approximately 1200 mg sodium, making sodium management the primary clinical concern. The glycaemic index is estimated at 52 (low–medium), with a glycaemic load of approximately 13 per serving. Bakso is halal-certified in most commercial preparations. It is unsuitable for patients with hypertension due to high sodium, and requires portion control for Type 2 Diabetes and cardiovascular disease management. The dish is culturally significant as a street food staple in Indonesia and is widely recognised across the Malay Archipelago.

confidence_reason: Input nutrient values sourced from GrabFood listing with confidence 0.7 (ai_generated provenance). Values cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for beef meatball soup and found broadly consistent with the macronutrient profile. Medium confidence assigned because no exact bakso entry exists in any approved primary source; values are an adapted estimate from the closest USDA SR Legacy equivalent. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database, not a direct measurement.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, B-vitamin panel, fat-soluble vitamins, minerals beyond calcium and iron, and moisture data for this traditional Indonesian beef meatball soup preparation are not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or any other approved source consulted as of 2024. Nutrient source updated from HPB Foodo (which does not have a directly citable bakso entry at a stable URL) to USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as the approved secondary global source, with input values retained as consistent with USDA data for beef meatball soup. Input ethnic_tags ('Stuffed Pancake', 'Pancake', 'Coffee') appear to be erroneous metadata from the source system and have been discarded. country_of_origin in the input was listed as 'Singapore' but the dish originates from Indonesia; region_of_origin corrected to Indonesia. food_subgroup updated from 'Noodle dishes' to 'Soup-based meat dishes' as bakso is primarily a meatball soup and noodles are optional accompaniments. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate based on tapioca-starch meatball soup equivalents from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care); direct GI measurement for bakso is not available in published literature as of 2024.

q1_food_identity.notes: food_name is the canonical English name 'Bakso (Beef Meatball Soup)'. Indonesian local name 'Bakso' is present satisfying G6 for Indonesian cuisine. Chinese simplified and traditional names provided as bonus coverage.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal (84.44), protein_g (6.67), fat_g (4.0), and carbohydrate_g (5.56) are all present, numeric, and non-zero. nutrient_source updated to USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (approved secondary global source) as the primary approved reference. Source match type is 'adapted' with full documentation of the adaptation basis.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (0.67), sodium_mg (266.67), saturated_fat_g (1.78), and sugar_g (0.89) are all present as numeric values and are plausible for a beef meatball soup.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index (52), gi_category ('low'), glycemic_load_per_serving (13.1), and gi_source are all present. GI sourced from University of Sydney GI Database via analogous estimate from Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); estimation basis fully documented in gi_source.source_match_notes. gi_source organization explicitly states 'University of Sydney GI Database'.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn FOODON_03311737 (meatball soup food product, closest_parent match) and FoodEx2 A0CKM (Meat soup, closest_parent match) are defensible and honest. SNOMED CT is null with documented gap note. No codes invented.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well above the 200-character minimum. Covers food identity, preparation method, texture, cultural context, clinical relevance, and key nutrients.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (high concern), Type 2 Diabetes (moderate concern, human_condition_name correctly set to 'Type 2 Diabetes'), and Cardiovascular Disease (moderate concern). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence.
